text: The surgeon docks on the right-hand side and begins dissection in the left lower area, crossing over the midline slightly. The dissection focuses on the left retro-rectus space, keeping the epigastric vessels close to the rectus muscle while separating the posterior sheath from the edge of the left side of the hernia defect.
The surgeon observes a large gap in the posterior sheath, with possible attenuation of the semilunar line, indicating the area where a previous surgeon initiated the left-sided TAR flap.
The surgeon carefully dissects to remove the mesh from the rectus muscle, revealing a heavily scarred area that complicates the procedure.

The surgeon navigates along the medial edge of the rectus muscle, dissecting behind it to reach the opposite side of the gap. A free space is found, facilitating easier dissection. The peritoneum, protruding into the abdominal cavity from the superior part of the hernia, is removed to aid in posterior closure.

The surgeon observes a full disruption in the middle of the Progrit mesh, with a small bridge of mesh remaining. The surgeon excises this portion to create a common hernia defect and sends it for analysis. The surgeon attempts to stay on the medial edge of the rectus muscle but encounters difficulty in locating it.
The surgeon navigates through the hernia sac and scar plate to re-enter the retro-rectus space. They work to detach the mesh from the rectus muscle, encountering significant scarring in the process.

The surgeon carefully navigates the plane adjacent to the rectus muscle, avoiding damage while moving inferiorly to develop the space of Retzius in the midline of the hypogastrium. The epigastric vessels are kept close to the rectus muscle during this process.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the left side and decides to perform a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) to better define the anatomy near the epigastrium. The dissection proceeds towards the pubic tubercle, avoiding the inferior area as the hernia defect is located above the umbilicus.
The surgeon initiates a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side, dissecting laterally before returning medially.

The surgeon observes a neurovascular bundle descending within a scarred plane, while expressing concern about potential challenges in maintaining coverage if a large hole is created in the posterior sheath near the scarred flap where the mesh is located.
The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, staying close to the peritoneum and the transversus abdominis muscle, while addressing the scarred tissue and mesh placement.
The surgeon navigates a heavily scarred plane, carefully identifying and staying medial to a visible neurovascular bundle near the semilunar line.

The surgeon identifies the superior edge of the hernia defect and attempts to re-enter the proper surgical plane, navigating through scarred tissue while being cautious of the transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon navigates through a scarred plane, observing muscle movement indicative of cutting the transversus abdominis muscle. The incision is made slightly medial into the hernia defect, away from the edge of the rectus muscle, to gain more space for identifying the transversus abdominis, while maintaining proximity to the back of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon activates a surgical tool to assist in the dissection of a heavily scarred plane between the rectus muscle and the mesh. The dissection progresses with visible holes being made in the tissue, as the surgeon works at the lateral edge of the mesh.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from the back of the rectus muscle, revealing the semilunar line laterally. The dissection progresses more easily as the surgeon moves lateral to the mesh, aiming to connect the dissection plane with the previously started transversus abdominis release (TAR) inferiorly.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site, noting abnormal scarring attributed to prior surgery where the peritoneal flap was sutured closed. The transversus abdominis release is continued, with muscles visible within the scar plate being dissected.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the semilunar line, frequently checking the area to ensure proper identification of anatomical planes.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the back of the rectus muscle, navigating through scar tissue on the lateral portion near the mesh. The transversus abdominis muscle is identified, and dissection continues in the proper plane against the bare muscle. The area is less scarred, allowing the surgeon to work back medially, despite previous holes made in the posterior elements, including peritoneum and scar plate.

The surgeon observes the transversus abdominis muscle situated in the middle of the gap seen posteriorly, noting the absence of a true defect in the semilunar line or a lateral hernia, despite the line appearing wider than usual.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, maintaining proximity to the transversus abdominis muscle visible at the top of the screen, while avoiding injury to the underlying peritoneum and pre-transversalis fascia at the bottom of the screen.

The surgeon continues the dissection by working laterally and then returning medially, having developed a space above and below the transversus abdominis muscle. The tissue in this area is less scarred, allowing for easier manipulation.

The surgeon dissects widely and high above the costal margin, focusing on the area under the most tension. The semilunar line appears widened, and the surgeon is on the superior edge of the defect, dissecting beneath the ribs. The xiphoid process is visible, and the dissection connects from the right to the left side, with the diaphragm visible at the top of the screen.
The surgeon continues to dissect widely, approaching the diaphragm while carefully manipulating the sticky peritoneum to extend the dissection further down without applying excessive force.

The surgeon completes the dissection by extending inferiorly along the full length, ensuring a wide area for mesh overlap. The costal margin is visible, and the dissection reaches its superior extent, showing the completed area.
